fact: February 8 is exactly 39 days into the year, which means you've already lived through more than 10% of the entire year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, groundhogs are typically still deep in their winter sleep on this date, dreaming away in their cozy burrows while snow piles up above them.
Maple trees in cold northern climates look dormant right now because their sap is frozen solid like tree popsicles, waiting for warmer days to start flowing again!
The sun usually rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around February 8 in most northern places, adding up to gaining 14 whole minutes of daylight this week alone.
February 8 falls in the middle of Aquarius season, making it one of the most 'watery' days of the year according to the zodiac, even though it's winter!
In Antarctica, February 8 brings some of the warmest summer weather of the year, with temperatures that might actually climb above freezing for the first time in months.
Owls in snowy regions often have their easiest hunting nights around this time because small animals leave dark tracks in the white snow that show up like roadmaps from above.
The Earth is still moving pretty fast in its orbit around February 8, traveling about 1,000 miles closer to its average distance from the sun compared to early January.
In places like Australia, February 8 typically marks peak cicada season, when millions of these buzzing insects emerge from underground and make noise louder than lawn mowers.
February 8 sits exactly 46 days before the spring equinox, making it the perfect halfway point between the deepest winter and the return of equal day and night.
Polar bears in the Arctic are usually at their fluffiest on dates like February 8 because their winter coats have reached maximum thickness to survive the coldest months.
The moon phase around February 8 changes every year, but when it's a new moon on this date, the night sky becomes so dark you can see about 2,000 more stars than usual!
